diagrams.title: 0/1 Knapsack DP Table Structure
diagrams.description: Shows how the 2D DP table is organized with items as rows and capacity as columns
elements: Row 0: Base case (no items) = 0 for all w
Column 0: Base case (capacity 0) = 0 for all i
Cell K[i][w]: Max value with first i items and capacity w
Arrows showing two choices: exclude (up) or include (diagonal up-left)
diagrams.purpose: Understanding DP table filling logic and base cases

diagrams.title: Recurrence Decision Flow
diagrams.description: Decision tree showing at each item whether to include or exclude
elements: At item i, check if wt[i]<=w
If yes: max(exclude, include) = max(K[i-1][w], K[i-1][w-wt[i]]+val[i])
If no: only exclude option: K[i-1][w]
diagrams.purpose: Visualizing the choice at each step of DP

diagrams.title: Greedy Selection for Fractional Knapsack
diagrams.description: Shows items sorted by value/weight ratio being selected sequentially
elements: Bar chart of ratios in descending order
Show full item selection until capacity reached
Show fractional selection for last item
Step-by-step accumulation of value and weight
diagrams.purpose: Understanding Greedy approach and why it works for Fractional

diagrams.title: Greedy Failure Example for 0/1
diagrams.description: Visual counterexample showing Greedy fails for 0/1 Knapsack
elements: Greedy (any): takes A=60 or B=100 or C=120
Optimal: B alone gives 100 (all items exceed capacity together)

Better example needed: capacity=50, items: (60,1,ratio=60), (100,48,ratio=2.08), (100,49,ratio=2.04), (100,50,ratio=2) - Greedy picks item1, remaining capacity49 can only take item2 or 3 or 4=100 total160. But items 2+3+4 weight147>50 can't. Items 2+3=100+100=200 weight97>50. Item2+4=200 weight98>50. Item3+4=200 weight99>50. Actually all combos exceed. Wait example not good. Better: capacity=10, items: (50,5,ratio=10), (60,6,ratio=10), (100,10,ratio=10). Greedy picks any giving max100. But 50+60=110 weight11>10. All single items max=100. Optimal=100. Hmm. Better example: capacity=8, items: (6,5,ratio=1.2), (10,7,ratio≈1.43), (10,8,ratio=1.25), (10,6,ratio≈1.67). Greedy sorts by ratio: 10/6=1.67, 10/7=1.43, 10/8=1.25, 6/5=1.2. Takes 10(6) remaining2, can't take more=10. But optimal: 6+10=16 weight12>8. 6+10(7)=16 weight12>8. Can't. 10+10(8)=20 weight14>8. So 10 is optimal. Need better. Let me: capacity=10, items: (8,5,1.6), (10,6,1.67), (12,8,1.5). Greedy: 10(6)+8(5)=18 weight11>10, so only 10 or 12 or 8. 12 weight8: 12. 10+8 weight11>10. Optimal: 8+10=18? No 8+10 weight11>10. So max is 12 or 10 or 8 = 12. Actually try: items: (value,weight): (4,3), (5,4), (8,5), (10,6). Capacity=9. Greedy by ratio: 8/5=1.6, 10/6=1.67, 5/4=1.25, 4/3=1.33. Sort: 10(6), 8(5), 4(3), 5(4). Take 10(6), remaining3. Can take 4(3). Total=14. But optimal: 8+5=13 weight9, 8+4=12, 5+4=9 weight7, 10+4=14 weight9, 10+5=15 weight10>9, 10+8=18 weight11>9. So 14 is optimal! Greedy works here too. Use classic counterexample: capacity=50, items: (60,10,ratio=6), (100,20,ratio=5), (120,30,ratio=4). Greedy picks 60(10)+100(20)+120(30)=all three = 280 weight60>50. Not valid. Greedy picks 60+100=160 weight30 remaining20 can't take 120. But optimal: 100+120=220 weight50 valid. So greedy fails! Yes: Greedy picks highest ratio (60/10=6, 100/20=5, 120/30=4): takes 60+100=160. But optimal is 100+120=220.
diagrams.purpose: Understanding why Greedy approach doesn't work for 0/1 Knapsack
